Library

Flexibility connection agreement · review dimension

⟳

Contract evolution and reinforcement pathway

How will the agreement change as the network, project, and reinforcement plan evolve?

Understand the dimensionComment on this dimension

Clear definition

What this dimension means

Contract evolution sets review dates, reinforcement milestones, parameter updates, conversion to firm access, transferability, renewal, termination, and treatment of changes in network topology or project ownership.

A flexible connection may begin as a temporary solution but persist for many years. Without an explicit evolution pathway, temporary access can become permanent uncertainty or substitute for efficient reinforcement.

Design choices

Choices that must be made explicitly

A reviewer should be able to identify which option is selected, why it is selected, who decides it, and how it is implemented.

Review cycle

Event-based, annual, multi-year, or linked to planning milestones.

Firming pathway

Automatic, conditional, compensated, or subject to a new connection process.

Change rights

How DSO and user may change capacity, control technology, or operating rules.

Transfer and exit

Assignment, refinancing, asset sale, withdrawal, termination, and stranded-cost treatment.

Practical example

How the dimension appears in practice

A wind project accepts non-firm access until a substation reinforcement is commissioned. The agreement should define the expected date, consequences of delay, interim review, and automatic conversion after commissioning.

Review principle

The mechanism is credible only when the selected design can be explained in plain language and linked to a verifiable network, regulatory, commercial, or customer outcome.

Stakeholder implications

The same design choice creates different responsibilities and risks

DSO

Align the agreement with network planning and investment incentives.

Regulator

Prevent indefinite deferral of necessary reinforcement.

Developer or aggregator

Protect financing, transferability, and asset value.

Customer or FSP

Know how long restrictions may last and how exit works.

Questions for reviewers

What remains open?

  • What event triggers review?
  • Who bears reinforcement delay risk?
  • Can the DSO tighten conditions after signature?
  • How is the agreement transferred?
  • When must flexible access convert to firm access?

Evidence expected

What should support the decision?

  • Network development plan
  • Reinforcement cost-benefit analysis
  • Milestone and delay scenarios
  • Contract transferability review
  • Long-term curtailment forecast

Feedback on contract evolution and reinforcement pathway

What should be clarified, added, removed, or redesigned?

Please comment specifically on the definition, design choices, stakeholder responsibilities, implementation feasibility, evidence requirements, and interaction with the other flexibility acquisition mechanisms.

Submit structured feedback
